chunk_summary: This section outlines India-adapted minimum and ideal monitoring tiers for restrictive diets, emphasizing safe prescribing practices, tiered monitoring, and escalation criteria for therapeutic diets like ketogenic therapy and multi-food elimination diets.
learning_objectives: Understand the two-tiered monitoring framework for restrictive diets in India.
Identify baseline and follow-up requirements for Tier 1 and Tier 2 monitoring.
Recognize escalation criteria for transitioning from Tier 1 to Tier 2 monitoring.
Learn specific laboratory and anthropometric guidelines for high-risk diets.
key_facts: Tier 1 requires baseline anthropometry, a written diet plan, and caregiver education on danger signs.
Tier 2 includes dietitian-led calculations, structured food diaries, and protocolized labs at intervals (e.g., 1 month, then every 3 months in the first year).
Recheck weight and intake within 2 weeks for restrictive diets or 48–72 hours for inpatient high-risk metabolic diets.
Escalation criteria include downward crossing of growth centiles, food refusal, recurrent dehydration, or laboratory abnormalities.
Focused baseline labs for Tier 1 include CBC and ferritin for gluten-free diets, electrolytes and fasting lipids for ketogenic therapy, and fat-soluble vitamin status for cholestasis.
reference_quote: Optimal Clinical Management of Children Receiving Dietary Therapies for Epilepsy recommends structured monitoring at intervals, including monthly and quarterly labs during the first year for high-risk diets.
document_key_topics: India-adapted monitoring tiers for restrictive diets
Safe prescribing practices for therapeutic diets
